ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to fish-shaped cheese crackers; mapped to broader cracker class FOODON_03315091.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers crackers and crispbreads as a category; no specific Goldfish cracker entry exists. Broader class used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fish-shaped cheese crackers; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Goldfish crackers is approximately 30 g (about 55 pieces), providing roughly 143 kcal, 5.3 g protein, and 240 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Goldfish Crackers — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 18617 (Crackers, cheese, regular)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Foodo (cr_id F11080027, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cheese crackers (FDC ID 172749) to supplement the HPB record which does not report saturated fat separately. USDA reports approximately 4.0 g saturated fat per 100 g for cheese crackers of this type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on wheat crackers / refined wheat snack crackers
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Goldfish crackers (any flavour) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour crackers / cheese crackers reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists wheat crackers at GI ~67 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (67 × 20.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.4. Available carbohydrate = 66.67 − 3.33 (fibre) = 63.34 g per 100 g; per 30 g serving = 19.0 g; GL = (67 × 19.0) / 100 ≈ 12.7, rounded to 13.4 using total carbohydrate basis for conservatism.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 800 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Goldfish crackers; even a 30 g serving delivers 240 mg sodium, representing approximately 10–16% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2000 mg, and regular consumption will substantially elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (66.7 g/100 g) with a medium-to-high GI (~67) produces significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit or avoid Goldfish crackers; the high refined carbohydrate density and estimated GI of 67 generate a glycemic load of approximately 13 per 30 g serving, causing meaningful postprandial glucose spikes that complicate glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (800 mg/100 g) combined with ultra-processed formulation and saturated fat (~4 g/100 g) elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Goldfish crackers due to the high sodium content driving hypertension risk, the saturated fat contribution from cheese, and the ultra-processed nature of the product which is independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 800 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; refined carbohydrate base drives pro-inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Goldfish crackers; the high sodium content worsens RA-related oedema and joint inflammation, and the refined wheat flour base contributes to systemic inflammatory load with no compensating anti-inflammatory nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goldfish crackers are small, fish-shaped baked snack crackers produced by Pepperidge Farm, available in multiple flavours including original cheddar, pretzel, pizza, and various seasonal varieties. They are made primarily from enriched wheat flour, cheddar cheese, vegetable oil, and salt, baked until crisp and lightly golden. The texture is crunchy and light, with a savoury, mildly cheesy flavour that makes them popular as a children's snack and casual adult snack food. Nutritionally, they are energy-dense at 478 kcal per 100 g, with a high refined carbohydrate content of 66.7 g/100 g and a very high sodium level of 800 mg/100 g. The fat content is 17.8 g/100 g with approximately 4 g saturated fat. Dietary fibre is modest at 3.3 g/100 g. Clinically, Goldfish crackers are unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium far exceeds safe thresholds), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined carbohydrate load with estimated GI ~67 causes significant postprandial glucose elevation), cardiovascular disease (high sodium plus saturated fat and ultra-processed formulation), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory sodium and refined carbohydrate profile). They are classified as ultra-processed food. A typical serving is approximately 30 g (about 55 pieces), delivering roughly 143 kcal and 240 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Goldfish crackers?
Are Goldfish crackers suitable for hypertension?
Can I eat Goldfish crackers if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11080027, confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central for cheese crackers. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct Goldfish cracker GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney database.
notes: calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Foodo source (cr_id F11080027). These values are confirmed zero per the HPB database entry; however, USDA data for similar cheese crackers suggests trace calcium may be present — the HPB value is preserved as the primary approved source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic Goldfish cracker record are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and were not available in sufficient detail from USDA FoodData Central for this specific generic formulation as of 2026-04-16. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'high_fibre'; this is noted but borderline — 3.33 g/100 g meets some definitions of moderate fibre but is not high fibre by strict clinical standards (typically ≥6 g/100 g). The high_sodium_warning tag is confirmed appropriate. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat cracker data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct Goldfish cracker GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
